preFix.title: Draught-sealing soil pipe and service penetrations through walls and floors
preFix.subtitle: Closing the gaps where pipes and cables enter and leave the building
preFix.excerpt: Every pipe and cable that passes through an external wall or floor brings with it an annular gap that admits cold air directly into the conditioned space. A 20 mm gap around a soil pipe stack exiting through a cavity wall can leak as much air per hour as a small open window. Sealing with fire-rated acoustic putty and a collar plate takes less than an hour per penetration.
preFix.sourceNotes: AECB Technical Guidance on airtightness detailing at penetrations. BS EN 15651 for facade sealants. gov.uk Building Regulations Part E acoustic sealant guidance.

content.text: Building services, the plumbing, electrical, and drainage installations, were designed for function, not
airtightness

. The gap around a soil pipe where it exits through an external wall is rarely smaller than 15 to 20 mm. A circular annular gap of 15 mm around a 110 mm soil pipe has an area of around 55 cm2, equivalent to a 75 mm diameter hole in the wall. Repeat this for every gas pipe entry, electricity cable, TV aerial, and boiler flue duct, and the total leakage area in a typical 1970s semi approaches 500 cm2.
Locating the gaps
Walk around the house and note every point where a service passes through an external wall or ground floor. Common locations:
Soil stack exit through the external wall (usually at ground floor level or below the kitchen)
Gas supply pipe entering through the external wall (typically at the meter cupboard)
Electricity supply cable entering through the wall or up through the floor from an underground service
Boiler flue duct through an external wall (two concentric ducts, the annular gap between outer duct and masonry)
TV aerial cable through the external wall
Cold water supply entering through the floor from below
Choosing the right sealant
For most penetrations, a fire-rated acoustic putty or a flexible silicone sealant rated for external use (BS EN 15651 facade grade) is the correct material. The key rules are:

Near a boiler flue: the zone within 150 mm of the flue opening can reach high temperatures. Use high-temperature silicone (rated to 250°C) at the wall face; do not seal directly to the flue duct itself without confirming manufacturer approval.
Plastic soil pipe through a fire-rated floor or wall: use an
intumescent collar
rated for the element (typically 30 or 60 minutes). Silicone alone is not adequate for fire-rated elements.
Gas pipe: do not apply sealant directly to gas pipes without checking with the gas supplier. The standard approach is to seal the wall aperture around the pipe, not the pipe itself.
Sealing a soil pipe exit
Cut back any old foam or hardened filler from the annular gap. Use a utility knife or a thin chisel. Clean the surfaces of dust and grease with a dry cloth.
If the gap is larger than 15 mm, pack with mineral wool to within 10 mm of the face. This gives the sealant a substrate to adhere to and improves the thermal performance of the seal.

Apply a bead of fire-rated silicone sealant to the inner face of the wall around the pipe. Tool the sealant into the gap with a wet finger and press firmly. On the external face, if accessible, apply a second bead and tool smooth.

Fit an escutcheon plate or collar over the pipe and bed it against the wall with a bead of external-grade sealant. The collar tidies the appearance and protects the sealant from UV on the exterior face.
What the saving looks like

Each well-sealed penetration reduces the total air leakage area of the building. For a house with a combined penetration leakage area of 200 cm2, sealing all penetrations to 5 cm2 or less can reduce the overall infiltration heat loss by 10 to 15 percent. The energy saving in a typical semi is 300 to 600 kWh per year. A modest saving in absolute terms but permanent for a one-hour job.
